text:
Epilepsy Centre

Neurosciences Division


From: Dr Emily Clarke (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Sent: 02 October 2025 10:15
To: kch.epilepsyclinic@nhs.net
Cc: Dr Naveen Shah (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST); Dr Rebecca O’Connor (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Subject: Follow-up review and management plan

Dear Dr Clarke,

Thank you for asking me to review Mr S, whom I saw today in the neurology clinic. He attended with his partner. There is a remote history of a mild traumatic brain injury sustained in adolescence without documented loss of consciousness; no neurosurgical intervention was required at the time. He does not drive and remains under standard seizure precautions.

Epilepsy Diagnosis:

Type not determined at present; classification remains under review.

Present Medication:

1. Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily
2. Topiramate 50 mg twice daily

Seizure Frequency and Semiology:

Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per year, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal myoclonic with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported. He and his partner describe brief pre-event light-headedness on some occasions, without consistent triggers. There is no clear diurnal pattern. No recent injuries have occurred. Post-event confusion typically lasts 10–15 minutes when events do occur.

Investigations to date:

- Prior routine EEG (2023) reportedly showed intermittent generalised spike-and-wave activity; the raw data were not available today for review.
- MRI brain (2022) was reported as structurally unremarkable, with no post-traumatic encephalomalacia.
- Baseline bloods (June 2025): FBC, U&E, LFTs within normal ranges. Vitamin D borderline low (55 nmol/L).

Patient-reported factors:

- Sleep is variable due to shift work; he notes worse cognition after night shifts.
- No alcohol or recreational drug use. Caffeine limited to one coffee daily.
- Uses a smartphone seizure diary; last recorded event was prior to the previous clinic visit.

Adherence and tolerability:

- Reports full adherence to lamotrigine and topiramate with alarms set on his phone.
- Side effects: intermittent word-finding difficulty and mild paraesthesia in the hands since commencing topiramate; no mood lability or rash.

Plan of Action:

1) Maintain current doses of lamotrigine and topiramate for now, given the absence of reported seizures since the last visit and tolerable side effects.
2) Arrange a 48–72 hour ambulatory EEG to better characterise the daily absences and myoclonic episodes and to support classification.
3) Request previous EEG raw data for internal review and obtain the MRI images for comparison.
4) Safety: continue standard seizure precautions, including bathing supervision where feasible and avoidance of heights without protection. He does not drive and understands to notify DVLA if there is any change in seizure pattern.
5) Lifestyle: encourage consistent sleep routine where possible; he will discuss rota adjustments with his employer to reduce overnight shifts.
6) Bone health: commence over-the-counter vitamin D (800 IU daily) and ensure dietary calcium adequacy; he will liaise with his GP for re-check in 3–4 months.
7) Follow-up in clinic in four months, or sooner if events recur, injuries occur, or side effects worsen. If ambulatory EEG captures events suggestive of a focal onset or another specific pattern, we will revisit classification and treatment options at that stage.

I have advised Mr S to continue using his seizure diary and to contact our team if there is any change in frequency, duration, or character of events.

With best wishes,

entities.text: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per year, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal myoclonic with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify all explicit seizure frequencies mentioned: (1) "one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per year" => 1 to 2 per year. (2) "He has daily absences" => daily implies approximately 1 or more per day; since it is the highest frequency dimension compared to yearly or every 3–4 weeks, this dominates per rule 7. (3) "focal myoclonic with disorientation every three to four weeks" => roughly 1 per 3 to 4 weeks, less frequent than daily. (4) "drop attacks" mentioned without explicit frequency. The note "Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported" does not establish ≥6 months seizure freedom, and elsewhere daily absences are stated, so patient is not seizure-free. Therefore select the highest frequency: daily absences => label as 1 per day.
reference: Seizure frequency has not altered significantly over the past six months; he continues to suffer one to two generalised tonic-clonic seizures per year, with a longest seizure-free interval of around three weeks. He has daily absences. In addition, he experiences focal myoclonic with disorientation every three to four weeks, together with drop attacks. Since the last clinic visit, no further seizures have been reported
